focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 11

In January the quill was still the same one he'd been using since October. A school quill, cheap, the vane split on one side where he'd pressed too hard during Babbling's mid-term assessment. He'd brought it to the sub-collection because it didn't matter — the exercise didn't require a good quill — and because he'd failed with it so many times that switching to a different one would have felt like superstition.

He set it on the stone shelf and sat back. The air in the sub-collection was cold and dry and smelled of limestone, the way it always did. Since the night he'd felt the give in the window frame under his hand, he'd also been unable to miss the slight give in the castle's magic, the sense that the wards were holding with less to spare than they had before. The walls gave off their faint luminescence — not torchlight, not Lumos-light, something older that came from the stone itself and cast no shadows. The manuscript lay open beside him, its notation shifting slowly on the not-parchment surface, but he wasn't reading it tonight. He'd memorized the relevant passages weeks ago. What the notation described wasn't complicated. The execution was.

The substrate around the quill was visible to him the way it had been visible since November — a structural quality in the air, not a glow or a shimmer but a kind of commitment. Everything in the room had it. The shelves, the stone, the air itself. The agreements that governed what things were and how they behaved, the rules that said stone was hard and air was transparent and quills were visible. He could see them the way you could see grain in wood once someone pointed it out. You couldn't unsee it afterward.

The agreement governing the quill's visibility was the one he'd been working on. Not because visibili...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ng that had once meant you were a student. His socks were visible below the hem of his robes as he climbed the stairs — Muggle socks, thin stripes of red and blue, the kind that came in plastic packets from shops that sold everything for a pound. The green jumper hung loose across his shoulders.

Gavin was alone in the common room.

The fire was ash now. Through the wall, plates clicked together, then quick little footsteps, then the scrape of something heavy being moved for Christmas breakfast a few hours from now. In the candlelight the windowsill plants looked black, all their leaves bent toward the glass.

He could still go after Duncan. The stairs had not had time to swallow all the sound of him yet. He could wake Miren and say it plainly before the approved form and the address and the fear got folded back into morning.

Instead he looked at his notebook. The passage he had been translating was still there in his cramped handwriting, four attempts at the same clause stacked on top of each other:

*The structure requires maintenance at intervals incompatible with the lifespans of its current inheritors.*

He'd written it differently each time and it kept coming back to the same bad point. Whoever built the wards had expected the next people to live long enough to keep hold of the whole job. Humans didn't. Gavin couldn't even hold the candle exercise steady for long without the pressure behind his eyes turning sharp.

He picked up his quill. He dipped it. He read the next line of notation and began the slow, wrong, necessary work of turning it into something his mind could hold.

Snow kept falling outside. The castle had gone quiet enough that he could hear the nib scratching at the page. Somewhere above the roofline, out past the dark, the wards were still holding.
